name: Parashu
description: The Parashu is a battle-axe, one of the most prominent weapons associated with several deities and heroes in Hindu mythology, most notably Lord Parashurama, the sixth avatar of Vishnu. This divine axe is often depicted as having a crescent-shaped blade, attached to a strong handle, and is a symbol of power, destruction, and righteousness. Its origins are often attributed to divine creation or bestowal, signifying its sacred nature and inherent power. The Parashu is not merely a weapon; it represents the ability to overcome obstacles, enforce justice, and annihilate evil. Lord Shiva is said to have given the Parashu to Parashurama after being impressed by his devotion and penance. The axe is believed to have immense destructive power, capable of cleaving through mountains and vanquishing formidable foes. It embodies both the destructive and protective aspects of the divine, representing the power to destroy negativity and uphold dharma. The significance of the Parashu extends beyond its martial capabilities. It symbolizes renunciation, courage, and the unwavering pursuit of truth. Parashurama's use of the Parashu to punish the Kshatriyas for their arrogance and abuse of power underscores its role as an instrument of divine justice. The axe serves as a reminder of the consequences of adharma and the importance of upholding righteousness in all aspects of life. It is a powerful symbol of divine authority and the ability to restore balance to the world.
subtitle: The Divine Battle-Axe of Hindu Lore
origin: Gifted by Lord Shiva or created through divine power
currentLocation: Mythologically held by deities or heroes, symbolically present

keyMyths.title: Parashurama Receives the Parashu
keyMyths.description: Parashurama received the Parashu from Lord Shiva as a reward for his unwavering devotion and rigorous penance. This divine axe became his signature weapon and a symbol of his power.

keyMyths.title: Parashurama's Vow
keyMyths.description: Parashurama used the Parashu to rid the earth of Kshatriyas twenty-one times as punishment for their unrighteousness and abuse of power. This act established the Parashu as a symbol of divine justice.

sources.author: Vettam Mani
